_meta.description: Mechanical, thermal, and chemical properties for FDM materials with design limits. Agent-consumable — structured for constraint reasoning, not human reference.

_data_quality.update_cadence: annual for established materials (PLA, PETG, ABS), semi-annual for newer composites (CF-Nylon, PA6-GF, PET-CF)
_data_quality.methodology: Conservative/worst-case values used for composites where manufacturer specs vary. Mechanical values represent typical FDM-printed properties (not injection-molded datasheets). Design limits empirically validated where possible.

agent_guidance: DEFAULT RECOMMENDATION for beginners and when no specific requirements override it. Easiest to print, best surface finish, lowest cost.
Z-axis (layer direction) is ALWAYS the weakest axis. Orient parts so primary loads are perpendicular to layer lines.
PLA creeps under sustained load — NOT suitable for shelf brackets, clamps, spring clips, or anything under constant mechanical stress.
Glass transition at 60C means PLA deforms in hot cars, near windows in direct sunlight, dishwashers, or near heat sources.
Snap fits will break after 2-3 cycles. For anything that clips/unclips repeatedly, use PETG or Nylon.
Good for: prototypes, display models, toys, cosplay, decorative objects, architectural models, teaching aids.
Bad for: outdoor use, mechanical parts, anything near heat, anything under sustained load, anything that flexes.
THE WORKHORSE. When PLA isn't strong enough and you don't need an enclosure, PETG is the answer.
Excellent layer adhesion makes it the best choice for watertight prints and functional parts under moderate stress.
Higher elongation (23% vs PLA's 6%) means snap fits survive repeated use. Good for enclosures, clips, and latches.
Stringing is worse than PLA — increase retraction, reduce travel speed, or accept minor post-processing.
Slightly worse overhang performance than PLA. Design with 50-55 degree max overhangs or add supports.
Food safe (PETG is the same polymer family as water bottles). Good for kitchen tools, containers, cookie cutters.
Good for: functional parts, snap-fit enclosures, food-contact items, water-tight containers, outdoor-tolerant parts.
Bad for: high-temp environments (> 65C), parts requiring high dimensional precision, cosmetic parts where stringing matters.
REQUIRES ENCLOSURE. Do not recommend unless printer has an enclosed build chamber. Warping is severe without one.
Best impact resistance of common materials — LEGO is made from ABS. Good for parts that get dropped or hit.
Acetone smoothing is possible for cosmetic finish — dip or vapor smooth for a glossy, injection-molded look.
Emits fumes during printing — recommend ventilation. Not suitable for poorly ventilated spaces.
Higher heat resistance than PLA/PETG — survives in cars, near electronics, in warm environments.
UV degrades ABS. For outdoor use, prefer ASA instead (same properties, UV stable).
Good for: impact-resistant parts, automotive, enclosures, heat-tolerant fixtures, mechanical parts.
Bad for: outdoor (UV), food contact, printers without enclosure, poorly ventilated rooms.
THE FLEXIBLE MATERIAL. Use for anything that needs to bend, flex, compress, or absorb impact.
Requires direct drive extruder for reliable printing. Bowden tube setups (like some Enders) will struggle or fail.
Print SLOW (15-25 mm/s). Flexible filament buckles in the extruder at higher speeds.
Dimensional accuracy is poor — TPU squishes. Add 0.5mm tolerance on mating surfaces. Forget tight tolerances.
Living hinges are viable in TPU — one of the few FDM materials where a printed hinge lasts thousands of cycles.
Excellent for vibration damping — motor mounts, anti-vibration feet, shock absorbers.
Good for: phone cases, gaskets, seals, flexible joints, vibration dampeners, shoe insoles, protective covers, grip surfaces.
Bad for: structural parts, precise fitments, gears, anything requiring rigidity.
THE OUTDOOR MATERIAL. ABS but UV-stable. First choice for anything exposed to sunlight.
REQUIRES ENCLOSURE. Same warping tendency as ABS — enclosed build chamber is mandatory.
UV resistance is the key differentiator — parts hold color and strength for years in direct sunlight.
Same fume concerns as ABS — print with ventilation.
Slightly better layer adhesion than ABS, slightly less impact resistance. Net win for most outdoor applications.
Good for: garden fixtures, outdoor signs, planters, car accessories, weatherproof enclosures, exterior mounting hardware.
Bad for: indoor-only parts (use PETG instead — easier to print), food contact, printers without enclosure.
THE ENGINEERING MATERIAL. Highest overall mechanical performance of common FDM materials.
REQUIRES ENCLOSURE AND DRY FILAMENT. Nylon absorbs moisture aggressively — must be dried before printing and ideally printed from a dry box.
Excellent fatigue resistance — gears, hinges, snap fits, anything with repeated mechanical cycles.
Living hinges are viable in Nylon — flex life in the tens of thousands of cycles.
Self-lubricating surface — excellent for gears, bearings, sliding surfaces, and bushings.
Dimensional accuracy suffers from moisture absorption — parts swell. Design with 0.3-0.5mm extra clearance on critical dimensions.
Chemical resistant to oils, greases, fuels, and many solvents. Good for automotive and industrial environments.
Good for: gears, bearings, hinges, clips, jigs, fixtures, functional mechanical parts, tools, industrial prototypes.
Bad for: watertight parts (moisture absorption), cosmetic-critical parts, beginners, printers without enclosure or dry storage.
THE TOUGHEST MATERIAL. Bulletproof glass is made from polycarbonate. Highest impact resistance of any FDM material.
REQUIRES HIGH-TEMP PRINTER (270-310C hotend, 100-120C bed) AND ENCLOSURE. Most consumer printers cannot print PC.
Highest heat deflection temp of common materials — survives 120C+ continuous service. Use near engines, electronics, heat sources.
110% elongation at break — nearly unbreakable in impact. Parts deform rather than shatter.
Warping is extreme — even with enclosure, large flat parts are challenging. Design with rounded corners, fillets, avoid large flat surfaces.
Absorbs moisture — dry before printing, print from dry box.
Good for: safety equipment, high-temp fixtures, impact-resistant housings, industrial tooling, automotive under-hood parts.
Bad for: beginners, printers without high-temp hotend + enclosure, cosmetic parts, cost-sensitive projects ($45+/kg).
STIFFEST PLA VARIANT. ~15% chopped carbon fiber increases rigidity by ~70% over standard PLA. Use when parts must resist deflection.
REQUIRES HARDENED STEEL NOZZLE. Carbon fiber is extremely abrasive — a brass nozzle will wear out in hours. Hardened steel or ruby nozzle mandatory.
Very low elongation (~3%) means parts are BRITTLE. Stiffer but snaps under impact. Never use for parts that take sudden loads or drops.
Excellent dimensional stability — very low warping and shrinkage. Great for jigs, fixtures, camera mounts, drone frames where rigidity matters.
Matte surface finish with visible fiber texture. Cannot be smoothed like standard PLA. Looks industrial/technical, not glossy.
Layer adhesion is weaker than standard PLA due to fiber disruption. Increase wall count and use higher print temps (210-220C) for better bonding.
Good for: drone frames, camera mounts, jigs and fixtures, stiff brackets, lightweight structural parts. Bad for: snap fits, living hinges, impact parts, food contact.

_validation.source: eSun ePLA-CF, Polymaker PolyLite PLA-CF datasheets

cf_pla._notes: Well-characterized material. CF content varies 10-20% across brands; used 15% baseline. Mechanical values conservative — some brands claim higher tensile.

agent_guidance: STEP UP FROM CF-PLA. Better heat resistance and toughness than CF-PLA while retaining stiffness. Good middle-ground engineering composite.
REQUIRES HARDENED STEEL NOZZLE. Carbon fiber shreds brass nozzles within hours. Non-negotiable.
Better impact resistance than CF-PLA — PETG base gives some ductility. Still more brittle than plain PETG.
Handles ~75C continuous service — suitable for under-hood automotive brackets, electronics enclosures near heat sources.
Low warping tendency makes it easier to print large parts than ABS-based composites. Enclosed chamber helpful but not strictly required.
Moisture absorption is low but still present — store sealed with desiccant for best results.
Good for: functional brackets, electronics housings, automotive parts, tooling. Bad for: snap fits, flexible parts, food contact, cosmetic/glossy finish.

_validation.source: Prusament PETG-CF, Bambu Lab PETG-CF datasheets

cf_petg._notes: Standard ~15% CF loading. Values based on Prusament specs cross-referenced with Bambu Lab. Less variation across brands than CF-PLA.

agent_guidance: TOP-TIER ENGINEERING COMPOSITE. Closest FDM gets to injection-molded nylon. Extremely stiff and strong with good fatigue life.
REQUIRES: enclosed printer, hardened nozzle, dry box feeding, 260C+ hotend. Bambu X1C with AMS is ideal. Most budget printers cannot handle this.
MOISTURE IS THE ENEMY. PA-CF absorbs water aggressively. Print from a sealed dry box. Wet filament produces popping, stringing, and catastrophic layer adhesion failure.
Young's modulus ~8 GPa — stiffer than aluminum in some orientations. Use for jigs, fixtures, end-use tooling that replaces CNC parts.
Heat deflection at 150C makes this suitable for under-hood automotive, near-motor applications, and industrial environments.
Cost is high ($40-60/kg) and print difficulty is high. Only use when PLA/PETG/CF-PLA genuinely cannot meet the requirements.
Good for: end-use industrial parts, automotive components, high-temp fixtures, gears, tooling. Bad for: beginners, budget printers, cosmetic models, food contact.

_validation.source: Bambu Lab PA-CF, Polymaker PolyMide PA6-CF datasheets

cf_nylon._notes: Values based primarily on Bambu Lab PA-CF (their most popular engineering filament). Bambu and Polymaker specs align closely. Moisture sensitivity makes real-world performance variable.

agent_guidance: BUDGET CF ALTERNATIVE. PET-CF is cheaper than PETG-CF or PA-CF while offering solid stiffness. Good value engineering composite.
REQUIRES HARDENED STEEL NOZZLE. Like all carbon fiber filaments, brass nozzles will be destroyed.
Lower moisture sensitivity than PA-CF — easier to store and handle. Still benefits from dry storage but is more forgiving.
PET base gives decent chemical resistance and low warping. Easier to print than nylon-based composites.
Stiffness (~5 GPa) sits between CF-PLA and CF-PETG — a solid mid-range option when budget matters.
Print temps are higher than PETG (250-280C) — verify your hotend can handle this range.
Good for: cost-conscious stiff parts, electronics housings, brackets, replacement parts. Bad for: snap fits, flexible parts, food contact, high-temp (>70C) applications.

_validation.source: Bambu Lab PET-CF, eSun PET-CF datasheets

pet_cf._notes: PET-CF is newer to market with fewer datasheets. Tensile and Young's modulus well-documented; compressive strength and HDT extrapolated from PET base polymer data. Values may shift as more manufacturers publish specs.

agent_guidance: COSMETIC KING. Beautiful metallic/pearlescent sheen. The best-looking FDM filament. Use when visual appearance is the #1 priority.
WEAKER THAN STANDARD PLA (~20% lower tensile). The additives that create the sheen reduce mechanical strength. Never use for structural parts.
Print settings similar to standard PLA (190-230C). Slightly slower speeds (40-50mm/s) give the best surface quality.
The silk effect is most visible on curved surfaces and large flat areas. Small detailed parts don't showcase the finish well.
Layer lines are less visible than standard PLA due to the reflective surface — one of the few filaments where layer lines become a feature.
Not food safe — the silk additives are not certified for food contact. Use standard PLA for food-contact applications.
Good for: display models, vases, decorative objects, gifts, cosplay props, artistic prints. Bad for: anything structural, outdoor use, functional parts, food contact.

_validation.source: eSun Silk PLA, Polymaker PolyTerra Silk datasheets

silk_pla._notes: Silk additives vary significantly across brands. Mechanical properties 15-25% below standard PLA depending on brand. Used conservative estimates. Thermal properties match standard PLA closely.

agent_guidance: LOOKS AND FEELS LIKE WOOD. ~20% wood fiber fill. Can be sanded, stained, and finished like real wood. Unique aesthetic.
USE 0.6mm+ NOZZLE. Wood fibers clog 0.4mm nozzles frequently. A 0.6mm or 0.8mm nozzle is strongly recommended. Avoid nozzles below 0.5mm entirely.
WEAKEST MATERIAL IN THIS DATABASE. ~30% weaker than standard PLA. Purely decorative — never use for structural or load-bearing parts.
Vary print temperature between layers (185-215C) to create natural wood grain color variation. Higher temps produce darker 'rings'.
Absorbs moisture more than standard PLA due to wood fiber content. Store sealed. Wet wood PLA produces excessive stringing and rough surfaces.
Sandable and stainable — post-processing with wood stain produces remarkably realistic wood appearance.
Good for: decorative items, planters, picture frames, models, artistic pieces. Bad for: anything structural, outdoor use (swells with moisture), precision parts, food contact.

_validation.source: Hatchbox Wood PLA, eSun Wood PLA, Polymaker PolyWood datasheets

wood_pla._notes: Wood fiber content varies 15-30% across brands (used 20% baseline). Higher wood content = weaker parts. Flexural and compressive values extrapolated — few manufacturers publish these for wood fill.

agent_guidance: PRIMARY USE: DISSOLVABLE SUPPORT FOR ABS. HIPS dissolves in limonene (d-limonene citrus solvent). Pair with ABS for complex dual-extrusion prints.
REQUIRES ENCLOSURE. Prints like ABS — similar temps, similar warping. Ventilation recommended due to styrene fumes.
Good impact resistance despite low tensile strength — polystyrene base absorbs energy rather than transmitting it. Parts dent rather than crack.
Lightweight — lower density than most filaments. Good for large hollow parts where weight matters.
As a STANDALONE material it's mediocre — lower strength than PLA, needs enclosure like ABS, poor UV resistance. Only choose HIPS standalone if you specifically need its impact/weight properties.
Limonene dissolution takes 12-24 hours for full support removal. Warm limonene (35-40C) speeds the process significantly.
Good for: ABS support material, lightweight parts, impact-absorbing housings. Bad for: structural parts, outdoor use, standalone use when PLA/PETG would work.

_validation.source: eSun HIPS, Polymaker PolySmooth HIPS datasheets, MatWeb HIPS polymer data

hips._notes: Well-established material with decades of polymer data. FDM-specific values (design limits) based on community testing. Primarily used as ABS support material.

agent_guidance: SUPPORT MATERIAL ONLY. PVA dissolves in water. Pair with PLA or PETG for complex overhangs that need clean support removal.
EXTREMELY MOISTURE SENSITIVE. PVA absorbs water from ambient air within hours. MUST be stored in a sealed dry box and printed from dry box. This is non-negotiable.
Dissolves in warm tap water (40-60C) in 2-12 hours depending on volume. Agitation speeds dissolution. No special chemicals needed — just water.
Best paired with PLA (similar print temps). Can work with PETG but the temperature gap requires careful tuning of the dual-extrusion transition.
NEVER use as a structural material. PVA has no standalone use — it literally dissolves in water. Its only purpose is dissolvable support.
Expensive ($50-80/kg) and has a short shelf life once opened. Only buy what you need for specific dual-extrusion projects.
Good for: water-soluble supports for PLA/PETG. Bad for: literally everything else. Not a standalone material.

_validation.source: Ultimaker PVA, eSun PVA+, Polymaker PolySupport datasheets

pva._notes: Support-only material. Mechanical values irrelevant for end-use but included for schema completeness. Moisture sensitivity data well-documented across all brands.

agent_guidance: BEST OF BOTH WORLDS. Combines PC's heat resistance and impact strength with ABS's ease of printing. The automotive industry's go-to FDM material.
REQUIRES ENCLOSURE + HIGH TEMP HOTEND (250-280C). Warps significantly without an enclosed, heated chamber. Not for open-frame printers.
EXCELLENT impact resistance (40% elongation) — parts deform rather than shatter. Suitable for protective housings, bumpers, safety equipment.
Heat deflection at 110C — handles continuous automotive and industrial temperatures. Good for under-hood parts, electronics near heat sources.
Easier to print than pure PC while retaining most of its strength. If PC is too difficult, PC-ABS is the practical alternative.
Living hinges are viable due to high elongation — one of the few FDM materials that can handle repeated flexing at a fold line.
Good for: automotive parts, impact housings, snap-fit assemblies, high-temp enclosures, protective equipment. Bad for: beginners, open printers, cosmetic parts, food contact.
pc_abs._notes: Well-characterized industrial blend. Values based on Covestro Bayblend (OEM source) cross-referenced with Polymaker specs. Living hinge viability is theoretical based on elongation — limited FDM-specific testing.

agent_guidance: THE OUTDOOR MATERIAL. Enhanced UV stabilizers make this the #1 choice for parts exposed to direct sunlight. Retains color and strength for years outdoors.
Improvement over standard ASA — better UV stability, slightly improved layer adhesion, and more consistent color retention under weathering.
REQUIRES ENCLOSURE. Prints like ABS/ASA — moderate warping, fumes, and sensitivity to drafts. Enclosed heated chamber recommended.
UV resistance rated 'excellent' — parts survive multi-year direct sun exposure without significant yellowing or embrittlement. Standard ASA is 'good'; ASA+ is a step above.
Heat deflection at 100C means outdoor parts survive hot car interiors, direct sun on dark surfaces, and industrial outdoor environments.
Ventilation recommended — ASA produces styrene fumes during printing. Use in a well-ventilated area or with a filtration system.
Good for: outdoor signage, garden fixtures, automotive exterior trim, weatherproof housings, solar mount brackets. Bad for: beginners, open printers, food contact, indoor cosmetic models.

_validation.source: Polymaker PolyLite ASA, Prusament ASA datasheets

asa_plus._notes: ASA+ is a marketing term — no universal standard. Values represent enhanced ASA formulations (Polymaker, Prusament). Thermal values 5-10C above standard ASA based on manufacturer claims; independent verification limited.

agent_guidance: UPGRADED PLA. Toughness additives give ~10% stronger tensile, ~33% more elongation, and noticeably better impact resistance than standard PLA.
SAME EASE OF PRINTING AS PLA. No enclosure needed, low warping, similar temps (slightly higher: 205-230C). Beginners can use this with confidence.
Still has PLA's thermal weakness — glass transition ~60C means parts soften in hot cars and direct sun. Not an outdoor or high-temp material.
The default recommendation when a user asks for 'something stronger than PLA but just as easy to print'. This is that material.
Snap fits are viable (moderate rating) where standard PLA snaps would break. The extra elongation allows some flex before failure.
Food safety is 'conditional' — same as standard PLA, depends on specific brand certification and printing conditions.
Good for: stronger prototypes, functional PLA parts, snap fits, clips, phone cases, tool handles. Bad for: outdoor use, high-temp, heavy structural loads, parts that need PETG/ABS properties.

_validation.source: eSun PLA+, Polymaker PolyTerra PLA+, Overture PLA Pro datasheets

pla_plus._notes: PLA+ is not standardized — toughness additives vary wildly across brands. Mechanical improvement range is 5-30% over standard PLA. Used middle-ground values. Some brands' PLA+ is barely different from standard PLA.

agent_guidance: HIGH-CF PETG VARIANT. Higher carbon fiber loading (~20%) than standard CF-PETG — stiffer and lighter but more brittle.
REQUIRES HARDENED STEEL NOZZLE. Higher fiber content means even faster nozzle wear. 0.5mm+ nozzle recommended to avoid clogging.
The stiffest PETG variant available. Young's modulus ~4.8 GPa approaches CF-PLA territory but with better heat resistance.
Excellent dimensional accuracy — very low shrinkage and warping. Great for precision jigs, fixtures, and replacement parts where exact dimensions matter.
Lower elongation (~3%) than standard CF-PETG. The higher fiber loading trades impact resistance for stiffness — parts are rigid but snap under shock loads.
Print 10-15C hotter than standard CF-PETG (240-265C) due to higher fiber content. Slower speeds (30-40mm/s) improve surface quality.
Good for: precision jigs, stiff brackets, gears, dimensional-critical parts. Bad for: impact-prone parts, snap fits, flexible assemblies, food contact.

_validation.source: Prusament PETG-CF, Bambu Lab PETG-CF datasheets

petg_cf._notes: Differentiated from cf_petg as a higher CF loading (~20%) variant. Higher CF content means stiffer but more brittle. Some values extrapolated from scaling cf_petg properties — limited manufacturer data for high-CF PETG specifically.

agent_guidance: INDUSTRIAL WORKHORSE. PA6-GF is the FDM equivalent of injection-molded glass-filled nylon. Highest heat deflection (180C) of any material in this database.
REQUIRES: enclosed printer, hardened nozzle, dry box feeding, 260C+ hotend. Glass fiber is EXTREMELY abrasive — even harder on nozzles than carbon fiber.
MOISTURE IS CRITICAL. Like all nylons, PA6-GF absorbs moisture aggressively. Dry at 80C for 6+ hours before printing. Print from sealed dry box.
Heat deflection at 180C — survives near-engine automotive, industrial ovens, and extreme thermal environments. The highest continuous service temp in this material set.
Glass fiber gives better impact resistance than carbon fiber equivalents — parts are tough AND stiff, not just stiff. Better for parts that take occasional hits.
Surface finish is rough and matte. Not suitable for cosmetic parts. Post-processing options are limited — painting is the main option.
Good for: industrial end-use parts, high-temp fixtures, gears, bearings, automotive under-hood, tooling. Bad for: beginners, budget printers, cosmetic parts, quick prototypes.

_validation.source: Polymaker PolyMide PA6-GF, Bambu Lab PA6-GF datasheets

pa6_gf._notes: Glass fiber nylon is well-characterized industrially. FDM values conservative vs injection-molded specs. HDT of 180C is the high end — some FDM prints may show lower due to layer adhesion limitations. Glass fiber is even more abrasive than carbon fiber on nozzles.

agent_guidance: BEST SURFACE FINISH. Matte PLA hides layer lines better than any other PLA variant — the matte additive diffuses light across layer boundaries.
Matte finish hides layer lines better than any other PLA variant. Print at 210-220C for best matte effect. Lower temps may produce semi-matte finish.
Slightly weaker than standard PLA (~45 MPa vs 50 MPa tensile) due to the matte additives. Not significant for most applications.
Same thermal limitations as standard PLA — glass transition 60C, parts soften in hot cars and direct sun.
Low warping, excellent dimensional accuracy — same printability as standard PLA with wider temp range (190-230C).
Good for: display models, cosplay props, decorative objects, presentation prototypes, anything where visual quality matters most.
Bad for: structural parts, outdoor use, high-temp, anything where strength matters more than appearance.

_validation.source: Bambu Lab PLA Matte, eSun Matte PLA datasheets

pla_matte._notes: Matte additives reduce tensile strength ~10% vs standard PLA. Surface finish improvement is the primary differentiator. Thermal and chemical properties match standard PLA closely.

agent_guidance: HIGH-SPEED PETG. High-flow PETG can print at 2x standard PETG speed (up to 300mm/s on Bambu printers). Ideal for large functional parts where print time matters.
Use higher temps (240-250C) and lower retraction (2-3mm vs 4mm standard PETG). Fan speed 30-50% for best results.
Slightly weaker than standard PETG (~42 MPa vs 50 MPa tensile) — the flow-enhancing additives reduce strength marginally.
Less stringing than standard PETG due to improved flow characteristics. Still not as clean as PLA, but noticeably better than regular PETG.
Lower warping tendency than standard PETG — better for large flat parts. Bed adhesion remains excellent.
Same chemical and food-safety properties as standard PETG — PETG polymer family is inherently food-safe.
Good for: large functional parts, batch printing, enclosures, containers, anything where standard PETG works but faster.
Bad for: parts requiring maximum tensile strength (use standard PETG), precision parts at extreme speed.

_validation.source: Bambu Lab PETG-HF, eSun PETG-HF datasheets

petg_hf._notes: High-flow formulation trades ~15% tensile strength for 2x print speed capability. Flow additives also reduce stringing. Thermal properties marginally lower than standard PETG. Well-suited for Bambu high-speed printers.

agent_guidance: IMPACT-RESISTANT PLA. Use PLA Tough when you need PLA's ease of printing but with much better impact resistance. Parts survive drops that would shatter standard PLA.
Distinct from PLA+ — PLA Tough has significantly higher impact resistance (20-25 kJ/m2 vs PLA+'s ~8 kJ/m2). PLA+ improves tensile; PLA Tough improves toughness.
Lower stiffness than standard PLA (Young's modulus 2.5 vs 3.5 GPa) — parts flex more before breaking. This is by design, not a defect.
Same thermal weakness as standard PLA — glass transition 60C. Parts still soften in hot cars and direct sunlight.
Snap fits are viable and can handle repeated cycling. Good for phone cases, protective covers, functional clips.
Prints as easily as standard PLA — low warping, no enclosure needed, 200-230C nozzle temp.
Good for: phone cases, protective covers, functional clips that get dropped, tool handles, cases, bumpers, snap-fit assemblies.
Bad for: outdoor use, high-temp, heavy structural loads — still PLA at its core.

_validation.source: Bambu Lab PLA Tough, Polymaker PolyTerra PLA+, eSun ePLA-Tough datasheets

pla_tough._notes: Impact-modified PLA is distinct from PLA+ — different additive strategy targeting toughness over tensile strength. Impact resistance 4-5x standard PLA. Stiffness reduction is the trade-off. Some brands market these interchangeably, but the formulations differ.

agent_guidance: STANDARD FLEXIBLE FILAMENT. Shore 95A hardness — similar to a car tire or shoe sole. The most common TPU hardness for FDM printing.
MUST print slowly — 20-30mm/s maximum. Higher speeds cause filament buckling in the extruder. This is non-negotiable.
Minimal retraction — 0.5-1.5mm at very slow retract speed. Excessive retraction causes jams. Accept some stringing and post-process.
DIRECT DRIVE EXTRUDER REQUIRED. Bowden setups cannot push flexible filament through the tube — it will buckle and jam. Bambu A1 and X1 are direct drive and work well.
Excellent for repeated flexing — phone cases, gaskets, vibration dampeners, protective bumpers, living hinges, wearable items.
Poor overhang and bridging performance — design with minimal overhangs (40 deg max) and very short bridges (5mm max). Use supports generously.
Good for: phone cases, gaskets, seals, bumpers, vibration dampeners, grips, wearables, living hinges, protective covers.
Bad for: structural parts, precision parts, fast printing, Bowden extruder printers.

_validation.source: Bambu Lab TPU 95A, Overture TPU, NinjaTek NinjaFlex datasheets

tpu_95a._notes: Shore 95A is the standard TPU hardness for FDM. Mechanical values are approximate — TPU properties vary significantly with print speed, temperature, and layer adhesion. Elongation at break can exceed 500% in optimal conditions. Glass transition of -40C means TPU stays flexible in cold environments.

agent_guidance: EXTREMELY SOFT AND FLEXIBLE — like rubber. Shore 85A hardness, similar to a rubber band or soft eraser. Significantly softer than TPU 95A.
Print at 15-20mm/s MAXIMUM. Even slower than TPU 95A. The softer the filament, the more prone to buckling in the extruder.
Almost zero retraction — 0-0.5mm. Any more will jam. Accept heavy stringing and post-process with a heat gun or lighter.
DIRECT DRIVE EXTRUDER MANDATORY — even then, may need manual feeding assist through tight spots. Not all direct drive extruders handle 85A well.
Not for beginners. Requires patient tuning, slow speeds, and tolerance for stringing. Recommend starting with TPU 95A first.
Excellent for: soft-touch grips, gaskets requiring high compression, vibration isolation mounts, wearable cushioning, very flexible living hinges.
Poor dimensional accuracy — soft material deforms during printing. Design with generous tolerances (+/- 0.5mm minimum).
Good for: soft grips, compression gaskets, vibration isolators, wearable padding, cushioning inserts. Bad for: anything requiring precision, beginners, fast printing, Bowden extruders.

_validation.source: NinjaTek Chinchilla 85A, SainSmart 85A TPU, Recreus Filaflex datasheets

tpu_85a._notes: Shore 85A TPU has limited manufacturer datasheets for FDM-specific properties. Mechanical values are approximate and vary more than 95A across brands. Flexural and compressive values extrapolated from Shore hardness scaling. Extremely difficult to print — not recommended for novice users or budget printers.
